abstract: Human-AI interaction in text production increases complexity in authorship. In two empirical studies (n1 = 30 & n2 = 96), we investigate authorship and ownership in human-AI collaboration for personalized language generation. We show anAI Ghostwriter Effect: Users do not consider themselves the owners and authors of AI-generated text but refrain from publicly declaring AI authorship. Personalization of AI-generated texts did not impact theAI Ghostwriter Effect, and higher levels of participants’ influence on texts increased their sense of ownership. Participants were more likely to attribute ownership to supposedly human ghostwriters than AI ghostwriters, resulting in a higher ownership-authorship discrepancy for human ghostwriters. Rationalizations for authorship in AI ghostwriters and human ghostwriters were similar. We discuss how our findings relate to psychological ownership and human-AI interaction to lay the foundations for adapting authorship frameworks and user interfaces in AI in text-generation tasks.

ur.ai_reason: 两实证研究用户对AI生成文本的所有权/作者身份感知
ur.tldr: 两项实验（n1=30, n2=96）探讨人-AI协作中AI生成文本的所有权与作者身份。发现“AI幽灵写手效应”：用户不认为自己是AI生成文本的所有者，却仍自我声明为作者。个性化程度不影响该效应，用户对文本的影响越大，感知所有权越高。相比人类代笔，用户对AI代笔的所有权感更低，导致更高的所有权-作者身份差异。理性解释在两者之间相似。研究为心理所有权框架和AI写作界面的设计提供依据。

title: The AI Ghostwriter Effect: When Users do not Perceive Ownership of AI-Generated Text but Self-Declare as Authors
title_zh: AI幽灵写手效应：用户虽不认为拥有AI生成文本却自称为作者
